annotations.label: Spherical coordinate system

anchor.snippet: specifies a given point in three-dimensional space by using a distance and two angles

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes only the 2-D polar coordinate homeomorphism, not a 3-D spherical (distance + two angles) coordinate system.

annotations.label: Radial distance

anchor.snippet: the radial distance r along the line connecting the point to a fixed point called the origin

annotations.note: The radial distance is just the generic Euclidean norm/distance from the origin, which exists, but no spherical-coordinate radial coordinate is defined.

annotations.label: Polar angle

anchor.snippet: the polar angle θ between this radial line and a given polar axis

annotations.note: The unsigned angle between two vectors (arccos of normalized inner product) exists generally, but the spherical polar angle is not defined as such.

annotations.label: Azimuthal angle

anchor.snippet: the azimuthal angle φ , which is the angle of rotation of the radial line around the polar axis

annotations.note: The azimuth corresponds to the planar argument (Complex.arg) used as polarCoord's angular component, but no 3-D azimuthal coordinate exists.

annotations.label: Spherical polar coordinates

anchor.snippet: provide a coordinate system on a sphere , typically called the spherical polar coordinates

annotations.note: Only the 2-D polar coordinate chart exists; there is no spherical-coordinate chart on the sphere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reference plane

anchor.snippet: is called the reference plane (sometimes fundamental plane )

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of a reference/fundamental plane for spherical coordinates.

annotations.label: Elevation angle

anchor.snippet: the elevation angle (or altitude angle ), measured upward between the reference plane and the radial line

annotations.note: No angle-between-line-and-plane elevation coordinate is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Depression angle

anchor.snippet: The depression angle is the negative of the elevation angle

annotations.note: This is a sign convention on elevation, which Mathlib does not formalize.

annotations.label: Hyperspherical coordinate system

anchor.snippet: is then referred to as a hyperspherical coordinate system

annotations.note: No n-dimensional hyperspherical coordinate system is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Coordinate system setup

anchor.snippet: one must designate an origin point in space, O , and two orthogonal directions

annotations.note: Although orthonormal frames exist, this spherical-coordinate setup description has no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Radius

anchor.snippet: The radius or radial distance is the Euclidean distance from the origin O to P

annotations.note: The Euclidean distance from a point to the origin is fully formalized, but not as a named radial coordinate.

annotations.label: Inclination

anchor.snippet: The inclination (or polar angle ) is the signed angle from the zenith reference direction to the line segment OP

annotations.note: The generic angle between two vectors exists, but the inclination/polar coordinate from a zenith direction is not defined.

annotations.label: Azimuth

anchor.snippet: The azimuth (or azimuthal angle ) is the signed angle measured from the azimuth reference direction

annotations.note: The signed planar angle (Complex.arg) appears in polarCoord, but there is no spherical azimuth coordinate.

annotations.label: Sign of the azimuth

anchor.snippet: The sign of the azimuth is determined by designating the rotation that is the positive sense of turning about the zenith

annotations.note: This orientation convention for the azimuth is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elevation

anchor.snippet: The elevation is the signed angle from the x-y reference plane to the radial line segment OP

annotations.note: No signed elevation angle from a reference plane is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Position vector

anchor.snippet: the vector from the origin O to the point P is often called the position vector of P

annotations.note: The term position vector is just an element of the vector space (vsub of two points) and is not a named Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Non-uniqueness of coordinates

anchor.snippet: any single point has infinitely many equivalent spherical coordinates

annotations.note: There is no spherical coordinate map in Mathlib and hence no theorem about non-uniqueness of its coordinates.

annotations.label: Negative radial distance convention

anchor.snippet: It is convenient in many contexts to use negative radial distances

annotations.note: The convention of allowing negative radial distances is not formalized.

annotations.label: Range restriction for uniqueness

anchor.snippet: the user must restrict the range, aka interval , of each coordinate

annotations.note: The 2-D polarCoord restricts its target to (0,∞)×(−π,π) precisely to obtain a homeomorphism (unique coordinates), the planar analog of this idea.

annotations.label: Inclination–elevation range equivalence

anchor.snippet: the range (interval) for inclination is [0°, 180°] , which is equivalent to elevation range

annotations.note: No inclination/elevation coordinates or their ranges are defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sphere equation r = c

anchor.snippet: can be described in spherical coordinates by the simple equation r = c

annotations.note: Metric.sphere x ε = {y | dist y x = ε} is exactly the set described by r = c, generalized to any (pseudo)metric space.

annotations.label: Latitude (elevation angle)

anchor.snippet: the geographic coordinate system uses elevation angle (or latitude ), in the range

annotations.note: Latitude as a geographic coordinate is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Colatitude

anchor.snippet: is called colatitude in geography

annotations.note: Colatitude is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Longitude (azimuth angle)

anchor.snippet: The azimuth angle (or longitude ) of a given position on Earth, commonly denoted by λ

annotations.note: Longitude as a geographic coordinate is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cartesian to spherical conversion

anchor.snippet: can be obtained from its Cartesian coordinates ( x , y , z ) by the formulae

annotations.note: polarCoord gives the 2-D forward formula (x,y) ↦ (√(x²+y²), arg), but the 3-D Cartesian-to-spherical formulas are absent.

annotations.label: Spherical to Cartesian conversion

anchor.snippet: the Cartesian coordinates may be retrieved from the spherical coordinates

annotations.note: polarCoord.symm gives the 2-D inverse (r,θ) ↦ (r cos θ, r sin θ), but the 3-D spherical-to-Cartesian formula is not present.

anchors.section: Cartesian coordinates
anchors.snippet: the Cartesian coordinates may be retrieved from the spherical coordinates

annotations.label: Cylindrical to spherical conversion

anchor.snippet: may be converted into spherical coordinates ( central radius r , inclination θ , azimuth φ ), by the formulas

annotations.note: Neither cylindrical nor spherical coordinate systems are formalized, so this conversion is absent.

annotations.label: Spherical to cylindrical conversion

anchor.snippet: the spherical coordinates may be converted into cylindrical coordinates by the formulae

annotations.note: No spherical-to-cylindrical conversion exists since neither system is formalized.

annotations.label: Ellipsoid level set

anchor.snippet: Let P be an ellipsoid specified by the level set

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of an ellipsoid as a level set.

annotations.label: Modified spherical coordinates for ellipsoid

anchor.snippet: The modified spherical coordinates of a point in P in the ISO convention

annotations.note: Modified spherical coordinates parametrizing an ellipsoid are not formalized.

annotations.label: Ellipsoidal infinitesimal volume element

anchor.snippet: An infinitesimal volume element is given by

annotations.note: The ellipsoidal volume element formula is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.note: The spherical-coordinate line element ds² is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: The linear transformation to this right-handed coordinate triplet is a rotation matrix

annotations.note: The specific rotation matrix to the spherical orthonormal frame is not in Mathlib.

annotations.note: The relation between Cartesian and spherical unit vectors is not formalized.

annotations.note: The spherical surface element r² sin θ dθ dφ is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: The surface element on a constant-θ cone is not formalized.

annotations.label: Volume element

anchor.section: Integration and differentiation in spherical coordinates
anchor.snippet: The volume element spanning from r to r + d r , θ to θ + d θ , and φ to φ + d φ

annotations.note: The spherical volume element r² sin θ dr dθ dφ is absent; only the 2-D polar Jacobian (det_fderivPolarCoordSymm = r) exists.

annotations.label: Triple integral over R^3

anchor.section: Integration and differentiation in spherical coordinates
anchor.snippet: can be integrated over every point in R 3 by the triple integral

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the n-dimensional radius/sphere decomposition ∫ f(‖x‖) = c·∫ rⁿ⁻¹ f(r) dr, but not the explicit (r,θ,φ) spherical triple integral.

anchors.section: Integration and differentiation in spherical coordinates
anchors.snippet: can be integrated over every point in R 3 by the triple integral

annotations.label: Gradient and Laplacian

anchor.section: Integration and differentiation in spherical coordinates
anchor.snippet: leads to the following expressions for the gradient and Laplacian for scalar fields

annotations.note: The gradient and Laplacian (Mathlib.Analysis.InnerProductSpace.Laplacian) operators exist generally, but their spherical-coordinate expressions are not formalized.

anchors.section: Integration and differentiation in spherical coordinates
anchors.snippet: leads to the following expressions for the gradient and Laplacian for scalar fields

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\nabla f&={\partial f \over \partial r}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}+{1 \over r}{\partial f \over \partial \theta }{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}+{1 \over r\sin \theta }{\partial f \over \partial \varphi }{\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}},\\[8pt]\nabla ^{2}f&={1 \over r^{2}}{\partial  \over \partial r}\left(r^{2}{\partial f \over \partial r}\right)+{1 \over r^{2}\sin \theta }{\partial  \over \partial \theta }\left(\sin \theta {\partial f \over \partial \theta }\right)+{1 \over r^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta }{\partial ^{2}f \over \partial \varphi ^{2}}\\[8pt]&=\left({\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial r^{2}}}+{\frac {2}{r}}{\frac {\partial }{\partial r}}\right)f+{1 \over r^{2}\sin \theta }{\partial  \over \partial \theta }\left(\sin \theta {\frac {\partial }{\partial \theta }}\right)f+{\frac {1}{r^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta }}{\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial \varphi ^{2}}}f~,\\[8pt]\end{aligned}}}

annotations.label: Divergence and curl

anchor.section: Integration and differentiation in spherical coordinates
anchor.snippet: the following expressions for the divergence and curl of vector fields

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general divergence operator (only the divergence theorem inline) or curl operator, let alone spherical-coordinate forms.

anchors.section: Integration and differentiation in spherical coordinates
anchors.snippet: the following expressions for the divergence and curl of vector fields

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\nabla f&={\partial f \over \partial r}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}+{1 \over r}{\partial f \over \partial \theta }{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}+{1 \over r\sin \theta }{\partial f \over \partial \varphi }{\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}},\\[8pt]\nabla ^{2}f&={1 \over r^{2}}{\partial  \over \partial r}\left(r^{2}{\partial f \over \partial r}\right)+{1 \over r^{2}\sin \theta }{\partial  \over \partial \theta }\left(\sin \theta {\partial f \over \partial \theta }\right)+{1 \over r^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta }{\partial ^{2}f \over \partial \varphi ^{2}}\\[8pt]&=\left({\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial r^{2}}}+{\frac {2}{r}}{\frac {\partial }{\partial r}}\right)f+{1 \over r^{2}\sin \theta }{\partial  \over \partial \theta }\left(\sin \theta {\frac {\partial }{\partial \theta }}\right)f+{\frac {1}{r^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta }}{\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial \varphi ^{2}}}f~,\\[8pt]\end{aligned}}}
